_meta.purpose: Short primary-source document cards for the President's Desk Read the Document surface. Excerpts are capped for classroom use; sourceCritique records transcription and locator caveats.

categories.label: Confederacy in its own words
categories.blurb: Secession, constitutional, and late-war Confederate documents that openly name slavery, white supremacy, or the contradiction of Black Confederate enlistment.

categories.label: Union war power
categories.blurb: Federal documents that turned emancipation and Black military service into war policy.

categories.label: Black freedom struggle
categories.blurb: Black soldiers and recruiters arguing for freedom, service, pay, and dignity in their own words.

records.title: Cornerstone Speech
records.author: Alexander H. Stephens
records.verbatimExcerpt: the negro is not equal to the white man; that slavery subordination to the superior race is his natural and normal condition.
records.attribution: Repository: American Battlefield Trust and Teaching American History. Locator: ABT Cornerstone Speech transcript; Teaching American History Corner Stone Speech transcript.
records.sourceCritique: Excerpt uses the shared wording in the two web transcriptions. Teaching American History hyphenates the speech title as Corner Stone; that title variation is outside the quoted excerpt.
records.indictment: The Confederacy's vice president described the new government as founded on Black inequality and slavery.
records.catalystFrame: Before a shot was fired, Stephens told a Savannah audience that slavery and white supremacy were the Confederacy's foundation.

records.title: Mississippi Declaration of Secession
records.author: Mississippi secession convention
records.verbatimExcerpt: Our position is thoroughly identified with the institution of slavery-- the greatest material interest of the world.
records.attribution: Repository: Yale Avalon Project and American Battlefield Trust. Locator: Mississippi declaration, opening paragraph.
records.sourceCritique: The double hyphen and spacing are preserved from the online transcriptions. The document is a public secession declaration, not a later memoir.
records.indictment: Mississippi's own declaration identified slavery as the core interest behind secession.
records.catalystFrame: This is not an outside accusation; it is the state's public explanation for leaving the Union.

sources.title: A Declaration of the Immediate Causes which Induce and Justify the Secession of the State of Mississippi from the Federal Union
Declaration of the Immediate Causes Which Induce and Justify the Secession of South Carolina, Mississippi, Georgia, and Texas

records.title: Confederate Constitution
records.author: Confederate States constitutional convention
records.verbatimExcerpt: No bill of attainder, ex post facto law, or law denying or impairing the right of property in negro slaves shall be passed.
records.attribution: Repository: Yale Avalon Project and National Park Service. Locator: Confederate Constitution, Article I, Section 9, clause 4.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt follows the constitutional clause as transcribed by Avalon and quoted in the NPS Union Dissolved teaching PDF.
records.indictment: The Confederate national charter protected slave property explicitly.
records.catalystFrame: Confederate leaders did not merely tolerate slavery; they wrote protection for property in enslaved people into their constitution.

sources.title: Constitution of the Confederate States; March 11, 1861
Union Dissolved
sources.repository: National Park Service, Appomattox Court House National Historical Park

records.title: Stephen F. Hale to Governor Beriah Magoffin
records.author: Stephen F. Hale
records.verbatimExcerpt: all be degraded to a position of equality with free negroes, stand side by side with them at the polls, and fraternize in all the social relations of life
records.attribution: Repository: Teaching American History, CivilWarCauses.org, and UNC DocSouth. Locator: Hale letter to Kentucky governor Beriah Magoffin, December 27, 1860; OR Series IV, Vol. 1, pp. 4-15.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt starts after Hale's dash so the quoted fragment stays within the word cap while preserving his racial-equality panic language.
records.indictment: A secession commissioner tried to move Kentucky by warning that abolition would produce racial equality.
records.catalystFrame: The argument was not abstract federal theory; Hale appealed directly to white fear of Black citizenship and social equality.

sources.title: Stephen F. Hale to Governor Beriah Magoffin
Stephen F. Hale, Commissioner from Alabama to Kentucky
sources.locator: Transcription from Official Records, Series IV, Vol. 1, pp. 4-15

sources.title: The History and Debates of the Convention of the People of Alabama
sources.locator: William Russell Smith volume, Hale letter transcription

records.title: Howell Cobb to James A. Seddon
records.author: Howell Cobb
records.verbatimExcerpt: If slaves will make good soldiers our whole theory of slavery is wrong
records.attribution: Repository: Encyclopedia Virginia and Official Records. Locator: Cobb letter to Secretary of War James A. Seddon, January 8, 1865; OR Series IV, Vol. 3, pp. 1009-1010.
records.sourceCritique: Encyclopedia Virginia provides the online transcription; the Official Records locator identifies the wartime printed source. The excerpt omits the following hostile prediction to keep the focus on the ideological admission.
records.indictment: Cobb admitted that arming enslaved men would contradict the Confederacy's own theory of slavery.
records.catalystFrame: The late-war manpower crisis exposed the central contradiction: the Confederacy needed soldiers but rested its cause on enslaved people being unfit for citizenship and arms.

sources.title: Letter from Howell Cobb to James A. Seddon
The War of the Rebellion: Official Records

records.title: Emancipation Proclamation
records.author: Abraham Lincoln
records.verbatimExcerpt: such persons of suitable condition, will be received into the armed service of the United States
records.attribution: Repository: National Archives and Yale Avalon Project. Locator: final Emancipation Proclamation, military-service clause.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt is the short military-service clause; the surrounding sentence names forts, positions, stations, vessels, and other service.
records.indictment: The proclamation tied emancipation to Union military power and opened federal service to Black men.

records.title: Men of Color, To Arms!
records.author: Frederick Douglass
records.verbatimExcerpt: A war undertaken and brazenly carried on for the perpetual enslavement of colored men, calls logically and loudly for colored men to help suppress it.
records.attribution: Repository: House Divided Project and Teaching American History. Locator: Frederick Douglass, Men of Color, To Arms!, March 21, 1863.
records.sourceCritique: The National Park Service classroom PDF hyphenates 'perpetual' across a line; this card follows continuous web transcriptions from House Divided and Teaching American History.
records.indictment: Douglass framed Black enlistment as a logical answer to a war fought to perpetuate Black enslavement.

sources.title: Frederick Douglass, Men of Color, To Arms!

records.title: James Henry Gooding's equal-pay letter
records.author: James Henry Gooding
records.verbatimExcerpt: We have done a Soldiers Duty. Why cant we have a Soldiers pay?
records.attribution: Repository: University of Maryland Freedmen and Southern Society Project, National Park Service, and DocsTeach/National Archives. Locator: Corporal James Henry Gooding to Abraham Lincoln, September 28, 1863; NARA Record Group 94.
records.sourceCritique: Spelling and punctuation follow the University of Maryland transcription. NPS quotes normalized snippets; DocsTeach supplies the National Archives locator for the original letter.
records.indictment: Gooding demanded equal pay by grounding Black soldiers' claim in their actual service and sacrifice.

sources.title: Massachusetts Black Corporal to the President
sources.repository: University of Maryland, Freedmen and Southern Society Project
sources.locator: James Henry Gooding to Abraham Lincoln, September 28, 1863

sources.title: Corporal James H. Gooding
sources.locator: Biographical page with equal-pay letter excerpts

sources.title: James Henry Gooding, Black Soldier's Appeal to Lincoln for Equal Pay
sources.locator: Record Group 94, Records of the Adjutant General's Office
